sentences.text: My name is Saul Gershkowitz.
In an American, I spell it S-A-U-L.
G-E-R-S-H-K-O-W-I-T-Z.
Well, in Europe, they called me [INAUDIBLE].
Which in Hebrew was Bezalel.
Gershkowitz, the same way but it was spelled different.
G-E-R-C-Z-K-O-W-C-Z.
Bezalel.
No, no, no.
They-- in the school, they called me 87.
There was a, an animal.
We used to collect pictures of animals in the candies.
And the 87 was skunk.
[LAUGHS]
Yes.
I don't remember the date of birth.
But I know I was born.
I took my own date of birth after the pogrom in, in Berezne and when we left alone and when I had to come down to the camps.
So I-- I know that I was born between Pesach and Rosh Hashanah.
So that means I took in the middle the day, the day, July 6th.
Right now, I'm 70.
B-E-R-Z-N-O, Berzno.
That's where I was born.
As far as I know, my dad was born in Berezne.
My mother was born in Sarny, which it was about 40 miles south from Berezne.
1996, we're speaking of Poland.
Berezne was, when I was born, it was in Poland.
And it's-- in, in 1939, became Russia when Poland was divided into sections.
Germany took half of it.
And Russian came in to place.
It, well, Berezne was a city, which it was Wolin-- W-O-L-I-N-- which it's a state.
And the capital of Wolin was Rovno-- R-O-O-- umlaut. it's a--
W-I-N-O, Rovno.
Well, Rovno was a very big city.
Then it was Bialystok, which it was up north, and then Wilno, which it was up north, too.
Krakow was south of us.
To Berezne?
Rovno was the biggest city.
The next biggest was Sarny-- S-A-R-N-O.
No, no, no.
This was--
Bialystok-- no, no.
They, they were-- Bialystok was maybe 230 miles.
Rovno was about--
Bialystock was north.
Bialystock was north of Berezne.
Yes.
Yes.
When-- during the First World War, Berezne was Ukraine.
Right
And to Germany, to Austria-- well, Austria, as far as I know, used to be-- they still owned, you know-- our place in Rovno was Austria, too, at one time.
Well, we had-- I had three brothers and two sisters.
One brother died and then-- from a twin, which the twin was still alive.
Yeah, as an infant.
And the other one died in birth.
When after the war-- I mean, during, before the world war, world war, three brothers and two sisters in--
Including me, yes.
Yeah.
My dad was a builder.
And in the wintertime, he was a-- he did a lot of fancy carriages, wheels, all kinds of very fancy for, for a carriage, for a big, rich man if they needed.
Non-Jews.
Non-Jews.
Neighbor.
Neighbor of mine by the name of [INAUDIBLE] was actually, I would say, at that time a rich man.
He had-- used to supply all the flour and the corn and everything to bakeries.
And he was considered a rich man.
Berezne was about 7,000 to 9,000 people.
It was a fairly small town.
Yeah.
I was about 3,500 Jewish people.
About a third, more or less.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Sometimes half to a third.
Uh-huh.
And what was Jewish life-- what was your life like in Berezne as a, as a boy?
In Poland, it was normal life.
My father worked.
We had a nice home.
We had a barn.
And we had a shop for my dad.
My--
The wintertime, he had a, a lay, a lay.
Which used to do the work.
He had a lot of carpentry machinery.
Most of it is hand.
And then we had a barn for two cows.
And we had a nice garden.
Yes, yes.
Yes, he had people working for him.
One of them saved me.
Yep.
Yep.
Daily life?
Well, in the morning, my mother used to go down and milk the cows.
My father used to go down and bring the water, because the water had to be take out from a--
Well.
Oh, about-- the well was about 200, 300 feet from the house.
No, it was outhouses.
We had two of them.
We were rich.
[LAUGHS]
Well, I-- we had breakfast.
And then I went to school, Polish school.
Breakfast?
Well, sometimes my mother used to make a [POLISH], which made of dough with a little bit of put honey or maybe a little jam and a glass of milk right from the cow.
The milk was still warm.
And then she used to make a sandwich.
And then we came home.
We-- when my father came home, we had supper together.
We walked.
There was no buses, no-- we walked.
School was about, I would say, 15 to 20 minutes' walk.
Winter, you walked.
You put on nice, big gloves.
And you walk.
Dirt
It was actually hard clay.
And the, the sidewalks are all made of wood, you know, planks.
Yes, oh, sure.
Yeah.
So you do the homework.
And you have supper.
And you go to bed.
[LAUGHS]
No, no.
We didn't have a television at that time.
No, it was a Polish school with plain teachers, no-- they had a Catholic schools separate.
But this was a Polish school.
Mixed Jewish, Gentiles, yeah.
And I used to go to cheder before, you know, before I had to have my bar mitzvah lessons.
Up to nine, 10 grades-- nine grades or 10 grades, I think.
Yeah.
Yeah.
We had, we had recess in school.
We used to play football, whatever.
And after school, we used to have get togethers with kids and play.
I didn't see anything between the children.
But we were on guard always, special before Easter.
We had to be on guard.
Because they have the, the services.
And they take out all the-- they carry all the plaques from the, from the goddess, from them.
And, and they told us not to go out on the street at that time.
The icons, yeah.
My parents, yes.
No.
Because it could be arrest.
You know, arrest some people.
They, they tried to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] start fights.
But as far as I know, we, we didn't go out.
And till 1939, we-- I don't think it was too much anti-Semitism.
It was anti-Semitism [INAUDIBLE].
But I didn't know about it, because I was young.
Yeah
Well, my dad was religious.
He wasn't pious.
I mean, he didn't wear-- he wore tzitzits every day.
I mean, I know.
But there was more religious-- the rabbi and his, his followers were much more religious than my father.
But--
My mother was religious, yes.
My mother came from a rabbinical family.
And--
Yeah.
She was from Sarny.
Yeah, yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
I never met any grandparents.
When I was born, my grandparents were already died.
The only one we had is my mother's sister who lived in [INAUDIBLE].
And I know that my. my father's sister lived with us.
And my father's brother and sister lived in America.
Yes.
They went, I think, in 1918.
Well, they went to America.
Well, I know that my father had papers to come to America because a cousin of his by the name of [INAUDIBLE] send him the papers.
And he never wanted to go by himself.
And he didn't have papers for the-- for us and, and, and my mother.
So he never went.
At the when, when that cousin, [INAUDIBLE], which he was a very successful businessman in America, was there for a few years, he did send them, I think, in 1920, '21.
Yeah.
Well, he, he's supposed to go by himself and then bring, then bring the family.
And I don't think he wanted that.
The first sign is-- was 1939 in the, in the summer of 1939.
Oh, we were, we, we, we, we heard that Germany is, is-- took over Austria, took over Czechoslovakia.
And they may, they may take over Poland.
But we were way up north next to the Russian border, not far from Ukraine, which it was Russia at that time.
And we saw a lot of people, Jews, who flew-- who came back from, from south, like Warsaw and-- to, to, to up north.
And we heard stories about killings and pogroms.
But you know, you always think that it's not going to happen to you.
And till 1939, when they made up a pact with Germany and Russia, that Germany will take half of Poland and Russia will take half of Poland.
And I was under the Russian-- city came under the Russian.
Well, frightened-- yes or no-- a little bit frightened.
Because if you have, if you, you know, you were disturbed in your life, it's frightened.
But you couldn't do anything.
You thank God that you were under the Russian mandate.
So it won't be any killing [INAUDIBLE].
Well, lately, you hear that, that they, they were causing a lot of, a lot of bad things for the Jews.
Well, they used to cut beads from the old Jewish people.
They used to make fun of them in, in the middle of the street used to beat them.
Heard, yes.
I, yeah.
Not, no, not at that time, no.
Polish people were anti-Semites, Polish people.
In the big cities, you can hear that happens a lot between the Polish people and the Jews, anti-Semitic wise.
In our city, small city, most of it was-- very few Polish people there.
Most of them were Ukrainian.
The only one who was Polish is the guy who owned the whole city.
And all the property was his.
Well, he was-- he was left with-- his grandfather and grandfather, they used to own hundreds of acres of land in the cities.
They build the churches and everything.
They used to own it.
And then the people used to work for them.
And they gave him so much back.
My father bought the, the--
Yeah, the, the land-- he had to go to that man and to, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to agree on a price.
And then he pay him, buy the land.
Noble families, yes.
Well, most of the time, we spoke Yiddish.
Polish, they made us-- all the teachers made us speak Polish while we were in the Polish school.
We used to have to put out the hand.
And she, she took a piece of wood and-- because we spoke Yiddish to, to-- between us instead of Polish.
We-- the only thing we hear that Germany is starting to take over a lot of countries.
And that's all we heard.
I was, at that time in 1939, I was 14 years old.
Yes.
A year after the bar mitvah, yes.
Yeah.
Yeah.
The Berezne shul.
It was a building, which its-- had a women's section upstairs and a man's a big hall with, with a nice altar with the, with the [INAUDIBLE].
Torahs.
We had a very, very famous rabbi by the name of Gedalche.
Gedalche is G-E-D-A-L-C-H-E, I think.
Gedalche, yeah.
No-- very famous.
We never talk-- we never said the first name.
His name was Rabbi Gedalche.
And--
Yeah.
Yeah.
As a matter of fact, I know that he came to Boston to raise money for a certain Jewish shul or something.
I think he came down there in 1937 or '38.
Oh, yeah.
He came back.
The Russians just-- they didn't have no opposition from the Polish.
They just-- because the Polish army had only cavalry, because-- and they didn't-- they didn't put up any fight.
I remember when this war started, they, they took my, my brother and my brother-in-law to the army, brother George.
And the last thing we saw-- we know that after the war is over that he was in a prison in-- I mean, as a-- the Russians caught the whole army.
And they sent them to Siberia, all of the Polish soldiers.
Yeah.
Poles mobilized--
An army.
They took my brother and my brother-in-law as Polish.
Polish.
And they, they had them somewhere.
And then the Russian army took him and took-- they didn't have too much fighting there.
And they took him in prison.
They send him to Siberia.
Yeah.
Well, my brother was-- after they let him out from the prison camp, they, they gave him work in Russia.
And he was in a Siberian city by the name of [INAUDIBLE].
It was, I think, 60 or 80 miles from, from Moscow, working in a hospital.
And how did I-- how did we met him is-- I'll tell you later the story.
But my brother, who was wounded in, in, in the Russian army, my younger brother-- I mean, my older brother but younger than the brother who was [INAUDIBLE].
And when he was wounded, he was in the same hospital.
And when my brother came in and he looked at the names which had came in, and he saw Julius Gerszowicz.
And that's how he met him.
Yeah.
This was in 1945.
Before the end of the war.
'45, yes, yeah.
Well, this was, this was in 1941.
But up till then, it was a normal life.
And then the Russians-- I mean, they, they took some of the businesses away from the big-- from the rich people.
And they made it like stores for which everybody could, could go if he worked for the state.
My father was workin', I mean, for himself.
He still had people workin'.
It was-- we went to school.
They reduced my schooling from seven grade to six grade, because the Russian schools were better than the Polish schools.
And we started to learn Russian.
And it was going on normal for two years, till the start of the German war with the Russians.
Yeah.
Was in the fall, in the fall, 1941.
Well, the battle was actually-- and the Germans were on one side.
And the Russians on the other side of Berezne.
And when we went out-- we ran out of the house.
And my father made, made us all lay in the ditch.
Yes, a lot of shooting-- and I would say 2/3 of the city burned down.
Our house was burned down, too, at that time.
Overnight.
I mean, the fight was going around maybe for six hours.
I think was August.
And at that time, the morning the Russian were gone.
And the German army startin' come in to our city.
Yes, artillery and rifle shots and machine guns.
And the citizens, the everyday citizens, just sort of froze.
They had nowhere to go.
Nowhere to go.
You are in between.
You on a battlefield.
You are there on the line, worse that them.
They had rifles.
We lay there.
After, after the Germans started to come in and--
The fields-- well, we're laying.
We saw, we saw the, the, the, the, the bullets flying back and forth.
We hear them.
But we're scared.
And my, my mother was holding cover my daughter and me-- my, my sister and me.
And my father was the, with the other brother.
Because third brother was in the army.
